summary: DHH argues that managers must distinguish between two types of problems with new hires: engagement (collaboration, communication style) and core competency (technical skills). Engagement problems can often be fixed with clear, candid feedback since they stem from unstated assumptions. Core competency problems, however, cannot be solved through conversations or performance plans, because skills built over years cannot be elevated to a new level in months. He advises hiring people at levels where they're solidly qualified rather than stretching, and recommends a quick parting of ways when competency is the issue rather than dragging both parties through futile performance improvement processes.
key_points: Problems with new hires fall into two categories: competency (skills) and engagement (collaboration/communication), and the remedy differs completely
Engagement issues stem from unstated assumptions and can be fixed with radical candor and clear feedback
Core competency gaps built over years of career development cannot be closed in weeks or months through feedback alone
Performance improvement plans for competency issues are often bureaucratic theater that wastes everyone's time
Hiring someone at a stretch level of seniority rarely does them a favor — better to hire where they can dazzle and promote from strength
money_quotes: If the problem is with core competency – their technical skills – you can't expect to solve that with a stern conversation.

If a programmer or a designer or a writer spent the last four-five-six years of their career getting their skills to where they are now, it's delusional to think that they can lift them to an entirely new level in just a month or two or even six just because you asked them firmly.
Problems with core competency is the pit where good intentions go to die.
The impossible walks in the desert that don't have any realistic chance of leading out. Motions of bureaucracy conducted to produce a paper trail. The worst.
Better to have them come in where they're solidly qualified, can dazzle against the expectations they can surely best, and then push a promotion from a surge of confidence and goodwill.
You do neither you nor them any favors by sugarcoating or delaying the feedback.
key_insight: Managers must honestly distinguish between fixable engagement problems and unfixable competency gaps in new hires, because no amount of feedback or performance plans can rapidly close a skills deficit built over years.
